\[\widehat\psi_v^{\rm FO}=\mathbb P_{\mathcal E_v}\left[\widehat m_{1,v}(H_1)+\frac{A_1}{\widehat\pi_{1,v}(H_1)}\{\widehat D_{2,v}-\widehat m_{1,v}(H_1)\}\right],\qquad \widehat\psi^{\rm FO}=\frac13\sum_{v=1}^3\widehat\psi_v^{\rm FO}.\]
Uniformly over \(P\in\mathcal M\), this estimator satisfies
\[\sup_{P\in\mathcal M}E_P[(\widehat\psi^{\rm FO}-\psi(P))^2]^{1/2}\lesssim n^{-1/2}+n^{-(\gamma_1+\gamma_t)}+n^{-(\gamma_2+\gamma_b)}.\]
If, in addition, \(M>1\) and \(0<\underline q<1<\overline q<\infty\), sufficiently small fixed amplitudes put both fuzzy experiments in \(\mathcal M\), their product-mixture affinities are bounded below by a constant, and the regular submodel together with the two fuzzy experiments gives
\[\max\{n^{-1/2},n^{-r_1},n^{-r_2}\}\lesssim\mathfrak R_n(\mathcal M)\lesssim n^{-1/2}+n^{-(\gamma_1+\gamma_t)}+n^{-(\gamma_2+\gamma_b)}.\]
Thus \(\alpha_1+t\ge d/2\) and \(\alpha_2+\beta\ge d/2\) are necessary for root-\(n\) minimax risk under the qualified converse, whereas \(\gamma_1+\gamma_t\ge1/2\) and \(\gamma_2+\gamma_b\ge1/2\) are sufficient. These conditions do not coincide in general. Moreover, when \(s_2<d/2\), the allowed stage-two block construction has admissible dimension sequences for which, under an allowed constant propensity \(\pi_1\equiv p<1/2\), \(K_2\) is the zero kernel with probability tending to one. Hence all \(\Delta_{2,k}\) and \(\Delta_{T,p,r}\) vanish on that event. The frozen \(\widehat\psi^{\rm HOIF}\) therefore does not establish the missing sharp upper rate, minimax equality, or root-\(n\) if-and-only-if elbow.

Let \(\gamma_j=\alpha_j/(2\alpha_j+d)\) for \(j=1,2\), \(\gamma_b=\beta/(2\beta+d)\), and \(\gamma_t=t/(2t+d)\). For every nonempty \(\mathcal M=\mathcal M(\alpha_1,\alpha_2,\beta,t;M,c)\), define the following fixed-proportion cross-fitted first-order estimator. Split the sample into three folds \(F_1,F_2,F_3\) whose sizes differ by at most one and, for \(v=1,2,3\), cyclically set \(\mathcal T_v=F_v\), \(\mathcal B_v=F_{v+1}\), and \(\mathcal E_v=F_{v+2}\), with indices read modulo three. Let \(Z_k\) contain the first \(k\) boundary-corrected wavelets from a fixed orthonormal basis with regularity exceeding \(\max\{\alpha_1,\alpha_2,\beta,t\}\), and define
\[\operatorname{LS}_{k,[a,b]}(W\mid X,S)(x)=\operatorname{clip}_{[a,b]}\!\left\{Z_k(x)^T\left(\sum_iS_iZ_k(X_i)Z_k(X_i)^T\right)^+\sum_iS_iZ_k(X_i)W_i\right\},\]
where the sums range over the stated training fold and \((\cdot)^+\) denotes the Moore--Penrose inverse. On \(\mathcal T_v\), with
\[k_{\pi_j}=\max\{1,\lfloor n^{d/(2\alpha_j+d)}\rfloor\},\qquad k_{m_2}=\max\{1,\lfloor n^{d/(2\beta+d)}\rfloor\},\]
fit \(\widehat\pi_{1,v}=\operatorname{LS}_{k_{\pi_1},[c/2,1-c/2]}(A_1\mid H_1,1)\), \(\widehat\pi_{2,v}=\operatorname{LS}_{k_{\pi_2},[c/2,1-c/2]}(A_2\mid H_2,A_1)\), and \(\widehat m_{2,v}=\operatorname{LS}_{k_{m_2},[-1,1]}(Y\mid H_2,A_1A_2)\). On \(\mathcal B_v\), form
\[\widehat D_{2,v}=\widehat m_{2,v}(H_2)+\frac{A_2}{\widehat\pi_{2,v}(H_2)}\{Y-\widehat m_{2,v}(H_2)\}\]
and fit \(\widehat m_{1,v}=\operatorname{LS}_{k_{m_1},[-L_c,L_c]}(\widehat D_{2,v}\mid H_1,A_1)\), where \(k_{m_1}=\max\{1,\lfloor n^{d/(2t+d)}\rfloor\}\) and \(L_c=1+4/c\). Define
\[\widehat\psi_v^{\rm FO}=\mathbb P_{\mathcal E_v}\left[\widehat m_{1,v}(H_1)+\frac{A_1}{\widehat\pi_{1,v}(H_1)}\{\widehat D_{2,v}-\widehat m_{1,v}(H_1)\}\right],\qquad \widehat\psi^{\rm FO}=\frac13\sum_{v=1}^3\widehat\psi_v^{\rm FO}.\]
Uniformly over \(P\in\mathcal M\), this estimator satisfies
\[\sup_{P\in\mathcal M}E_P[(\widehat\psi^{\rm FO}-\psi(P))^2]^{1/2}\lesssim n^{-1/2}+n^{-(\gamma_1+\gamma_t)}+n^{-(\gamma_2+\gamma_b)}.\]
If, in addition, \(M>1\) and \(0<\underline q<1<\overline q<\infty\), sufficiently small fixed amplitudes put both fuzzy experiments in \(\mathcal M\), their product-mixture affinities are bounded below by a constant, and the regular submodel together with the two fuzzy experiments gives
\[\max\{n^{-1/2},n^{-r_1},n^{-r_2}\}\lesssim\mathfrak R_n(\mathcal M)\lesssim n^{-1/2}+n^{-(\gamma_1+\gamma_t)}+n^{-(\gamma_2+\gamma_b)}.\]
Thus, under the qualified converse, \(\alpha_1+t\ge d/2\) and \(\alpha_2+\beta\ge d/2\) are necessary for root-\(n\) minimax risk, whereas the first-order upper bound makes \(\gamma_1+\gamma_t\ge1/2\) and \(\gamma_2+\gamma_b\ge1/2\) sufficient. The \(n^{-r_1}\) term is the incoming-transition channel: it is generated by a sign-paired perturbation of \(\pi_1\) and the incoming-state \(C^t(L^1)\) transition law. The \(n^{-r_2}\) term is the standard single-time-type terminal channel, and no novelty is asserted for that component. The necessary and sufficient conditions do not coincide in general. Accordingly, this theorem establishes an explicit nonmatching model-specific bracket; it does not determine the minimax rate or a root-\(n\) if-and-only-if boundary.
changed.reason: The proved field-facing result is the qualified incoming-transition converse and explicit nonmatching first-order bracket. Removing the frozen-HOIF obstruction narrows the headline to that result, labels the terminal channel as standard, and leaves the minimax frontier and sharp elbow open.

Let \(u_{j,h}(x)=u((x-x_j)/h)\) be disjoint smooth mean-zero bumps and let \(v\) be bounded, analytic, mean-zero, and nonzero. Set \(p_1=1\), \(\pi_2=1/2\), \(m_2=\delta v\), \(J\asymp h^{-d}\), \(\eta=c_\eta h^{\alpha_1}\), \(\epsilon=c_\epsilon h^t\), \(\pi_{1,z}=1/2+\eta\sum_jz_ju_{j,h}\), and \(g_{z,\lambda}(h_2\mid h_1)=1+\epsilon\sum_j\lambda_ju_{j,h}(h_1)v(h_2)/\{1+2\eta z_ju_{j,h}(h_1)\}\). Set every untreated or off-regime transition to one, \(P(A_2=1\mid h_2,a_1)=1/2\), and \(P(Y=1\mid h_2,1,1)=\{1+\delta v(h_2)\}/2\), with all other conditional means zero. The observed factorization is \(p_1(h_1)p(a_1\mid h_1)p(h_2\mid h_1,a_1)p(a_2\mid h_2,a_1)p(y\mid h_2,a_1,a_2)\). Let \(
u_+\) mix uniformly over \(z\) with \(\lambda=z\), \(
u_-\) mix uniformly with \(\lambda=-z\), and \(\overline P_\pm=\int P_{z,\pm z}^{\otimes n}d
u_\pm(z)\). For \(2\eta\|u\|_\infty<1\), \(\epsilon\|u\|_\infty\|v\|_\infty<1-2\eta\|u\|_\infty\), and \(\delta\|v\|_\infty<1\), positivity, normalization, and binary validity hold. Moreover \(\pi_{1,z}g_{z,\lambda}=\pi_{1,z}+\epsilon\sum_j\lambda_ju_{j,h}v/2\), the exact target separation is \(4\eta\epsilon\delta(\int v^2)\sum_j\int u_{j,h}^2/(1-4\eta^2u_{j,h}^2)\asymp\eta\epsilon\), and with \(h\asymp n^{-2/\{2(\alpha_1+t)+d\}}\) the required affinity calculation is \(n^2\eta^2\epsilon^2/J=O(1)\).

Choose a nonzero \(C^\infty\) bump \(u\), supported on a reflection-symmetric cube, such that \(u\circ\tau=-u\) for the measure-preserving reflection \(\tau(x_1,x_{-1})=(-x_1,x_{-1})\). Let \(u_{j,h}(x)=u((x-x_j)/h)\) be disjoint translated and rescaled copies, and let \(v\) be bounded, analytic, mean-zero, and nonzero. Set \(p_1=1\), \(\pi_2=1/2\), \(m_2=\delta v\), \(J\asymp h^{-d}\), \(\eta=c_\eta h^{\alpha_1}\), \(\epsilon=c_\epsilon h^t\), \(\pi_{1,z}=1/2+\eta\sum_jz_ju_{j,h}\), and
\[
g_{z,\lambda}(h_2\mid h_1)=1+\epsilon\sum_j\frac{\lambda_ju_{j,h}(h_1)v(h_2)}{1+2\eta z_ju_{j,h}(h_1)}.
\]
Set every untreated or off-regime transition to one, \(P(A_2=1\mid h_2,a_1)=1/2\), and \(P(Y=1\mid h_2,1,1)=\{1+\delta v(h_2)\}/2\), with all other conditional means zero. The observed factorization is \(p_1(h_1)p(a_1\mid h_1)p(h_2\mid h_1,a_1)p(a_2\mid h_2,a_1)p(y\mid h_2,a_1,a_2)\). Independently draw \(U_{A1},U_{H2},U_{A2},U_Y^{a_1a_2}\), generate \(H_2(a_1)\) by inverse conditional distribution functions, threshold both treatments, set \(Y(a_1,a_2)=2\mathbf1\{U_Y^{a_1a_2}\le[1+\mu_{a_1a_2}(H_2(a_1))]/2\}-1\), and set \(H_2=H_2(A_1)\), \(Y=Y(A_1,A_2)\). Let \(\nu_+\) mix uniformly over \(z\) with \(\lambda=z\), let \(\nu_-\) mix uniformly with \(\lambda=-z\), and set \(\overline P_\pm=\int P_{z,\pm z}^{\otimes n}d\nu_\pm(z)\). For \(2\eta\|u\|_\infty<1\), \(\epsilon\|u\|_\infty\|v\|_\infty<1-2\eta\|u\|_\infty\), and \(\delta\|v\|_\infty<1\), positivity, normalization, and binary validity hold. Moreover,
\[
\pi_{1,z}g_{z,\lambda}=\pi_{1,z}+\frac{\epsilon}{2}\sum_j\lambda_ju_{j,h}v.
\]
Reflection symmetry gives
\[
\int\frac{z_ju_{j,h}}{1+2\eta z_ju_{j,h}}
=-2\eta\int\frac{u_{j,h}^2}{1-4\eta^2u_{j,h}^2},
\]
so the two mixing supports have the exact sign-independent target separation
\[
4\eta\epsilon\delta\left(\int v^2\right)\sum_j\int\frac{u_{j,h}^2}{1-4\eta^2u_{j,h}^2}\asymp\eta\epsilon.
\]
With \(h\asymp n^{-2/\{2(\alpha_1+t)+d\}}\), the affinity scale is \(n^2\eta^2\epsilon^2/J=O(1)\).
changed.reason: Mean zero alone does not yield the exact sign-independent denominator identity. Reflection symmetry of the bump gives the required identity with \(1-4\eta^2u^2\) and makes the two fuzzy target supports exactly sign-opposed.

For \(2a\|v_{j,h}\|_\infty<1\) and \(b_0\|v_{j,h}\|_\infty/(1-2a\|v_{j,h}\|_\infty)<1\), all probabilities are valid. With \(h\asymp n^{-2/\{2(\alpha_2+\beta)+d\}}\), the exact separation is \(4ab_0\sum_j\int v_{j,h}^2/(1-4a^2v_{j,h}^2)\asymp ab_0=n^{-r_2}\), and the required affinity calculation is \(n^2a^2b_0^2/J=O(1)\).
changed.to:
Fix \(p_1=1\), \(\pi_1=1/2\), and every transition density at one. Choose disjoint smooth localized bumps \(v_{j,h}\) whose reference bump is sign-symmetric under a measure-preserving reflection, so \(v_{j,h}\circ\tau_j=-v_{j,h}\). Set \(J\asymp h^{-d}\), \(a=c_ah^{\alpha_2}\), \(b_0=c_bh^\beta\),
\[
\pi_{2,z}=1/2+a\sum_jz_jv_{j,h},
\qquad
m_{2,z,\lambda}=b_0\sum_j\frac{\lambda_jv_{j,h}}{1+2az_jv_{j,h}}.
\]
Use the factorization, off-regime laws, independent-uniform full-data extension, and product mixtures specified in \(\texttt{def:first-fuzzy-experiment}\), replacing the terminal treated mean by \(m_{2,z,\lambda}\). For \(2a\|v_{j,h}\|_\infty<1\) and \(b_0\|v_{j,h}\|_\infty/(1-2a\|v_{j,h}\|_\infty)<1\), all probabilities are valid. Reflection symmetry gives
\[
\int\frac{z_jv_{j,h}}{1+2az_jv_{j,h}}
=-2a\int\frac{v_{j,h}^2}{1-4a^2v_{j,h}^2}.
\]
Thus, with \(h\asymp n^{-2/\{2(\alpha_2+\beta)+d\}}\), the two mixing supports have the exact sign-independent separation
\[
4ab_0\sum_j\int\frac{v_{j,h}^2}{1-4a^2v_{j,h}^2}\asymp ab_0=n^{-r_2},
\]
and the affinity scale is \(n^2a^2b_0^2/J=O(1)\).
changed.reason: The exact two-sided terminal separation with denominator \(1-4a^2v^2\) requires an explicit sign-symmetric bump, not merely a smooth localized bump.

g_{z,\lambda}(h_2\mid h_1)=1+\epsilon\sum_j\frac{\lambda_ju_{j,h}(h_1)v(h_2)}{1+2\eta z_ju_{j,h}(h_1)}.

Set every untreated or off-regime transition to one, \(P(A_2=1\mid h_2,a_1)=1/2\), and \(P(Y=1\mid h_2,1,1)=\{1+\delta v(h_2)\}/2\), with all other conditional means zero. \[
\pi_{1,z}g_{z,\lambda}=\pi_{1,z}+\frac{\epsilon}{2}\sum_j\lambda_ju_{j,h}v.
\]
Reflection symmetry gives
\[

=-2\eta\int\frac{u_{j,h}^2}{1-4\eta^2u_{j,h}^2},

so the two mixing supports have the exact sign-independent target separation
\[

{"id":"def:first-fuzzy-experiment","name":"period-one paired fuzzy experiment and rough comparator subclass","construction":"Choose a nonzero \(C^\infty\) reflection-odd bump \(u\) supported on a symmetric cube, with disjoint copies \(u_{j,h}\), \(J\asymp h^{-d}\), signs \(z,\lambda\in\{-1,1\}^J\), \(\eta=c_\eta h^{\alpha_1}\), and \(\epsilon=c_\epsilon h^t\). Put \(\pi_{1,z}=1/2+\eta\sum_jz_ju_{j,h}\), \(\pi_2=1/2\), and \(m_2=\delta v\). The flat-base family used by \(\texttt{thm:transition-mediated-converse-first-order-bracket}\) is
\[
g^{\rm flat}_{z,\lambda}(h_2\mid h_1)
=1+\epsilon\sum_j\frac{\lambda_ju_{j,h}(h_1)v(h_2)}
 {1+2\eta z_ju_{j,h}(h_1)}.
\]
Use transition density one and fair Rademacher outcomes off regime, and on \(A_1=A_2=1\) let \(P(Y=1\mid H_2)=\{1+\delta v(H_2)\}/2\). Generate the full-data extension from independent uniforms \(U_{A1},U_{H2},U_{A2},U_Y^{a_1a_2}\) by thresholding the treatment probabilities, inverse-transforming each conditional transition, defining the potential outcomes from the corresponding conditional means, and imposing \(H_2=H_2(A_1)\) and \(Y=Y(A_1,A_2)\). Let \(\nu_+\) mix uniformly over \(z\) with \(\lambda=z\), let \(\nu_-\) mix uniformly with \(\lambda=-z\), and define \(\overline P_\pm=\int P_{z,\pm z}^{\otimes n}d\nu_\pm(z)\).

For the same-subclass comparison, fix
\[
q(h_2)=\mathbf1\{h_{2,1}\le1/2\}-\mathbf1\{h_{2,1}>1/2\},\quad
a(h_1)=\cos(2\pi h_{1,1}),\quad
v(h_2)=\cos(4\pi h_{2,1}),
\]
extended constantly in the remaining coordinates, and fix small \(\kappa,\delta>0\). Define
\[
g_0(h_2\mid h_1)=1+\kappa a(h_1)q(h_2).
\]
With the same fixed off-regime and independent-uniform full-data laws, define the \(n\)-independent subclass
\[
\mathcal M_{\rm rough}
=\left\{P\in\mathcal M(\alpha_1,\alpha_2,\beta,t;M,c):
\begin{array}{l}
H_1\sim{\rm Unif}([0,1]^d),\ \pi_2=1/2,\ m_2=\delta v,\\
g=g_0+r,\ \int r(h_2\mid h_1)\,dh_2=0,\ 
h_2\mapsto r(h_2\mid h_1)\ {\rm is\ continuous},\\
(1-\kappa)/2\le g(h_2\mid h_1)\le2(1+\kappa)
\end{array}\right\}.
\]
The rough fuzzy transition is
\[
g^{\rm rough}_{z,\lambda}(h_2\mid h_1)
=g_0(h_2\mid h_1)+
\epsilon\sum_j\frac{\lambda_ju_{j,h}(h_1)v(h_2)}
 {1+2\eta z_ju_{j,h}(h_1)}.
\]
It uses the same \(\pi_{1,z}\), \(\pi_2\), \(m_2\), full-data construction, and mixtures \(\lambda=z\) versus \(\lambda=-z\). Relative to its common law with first propensity \(1/2\) and treated transition \(g_0\), define the cell channels
\[
F_j=2\eta(2A_1-1)u_{j,h}(H_1),\qquad
E_j=\epsilon\mathbf1\{A_1=1\}
\frac{u_{j,h}(H_1)v(H_2)}{g_0(H_2\mid H_1)}.
\]
For each \(P\in\mathcal M_{\rm rough}\), let
\[
K_P(dy\mid l_0,l_1)=P(Y\in dy\mid H_1=l_0,H_2=l_1,A_1=A_2=1)
\]
be a version of the treated-outcome regular conditional kernel. Such a kernel exists because the state and outcome spaces are standard Borel; strict overlap makes its treated-history domain equivalent to the \(p_1(h_1)g(h_2\mid h_1)\,dh_1dh_2\) domain, up to null sets. Define an auxiliary two-occasion full-data law by drawing \((L_0,L_1)\) with joint density \(p_1(h_1)g(h_2\mid h_1)\), then drawing \(Y^{\rm MAR}\) conditionally from \(K_P(\cdot\mid L_0,L_1)\). Conditional on the full data \((L_0,L_1,Y^{\rm MAR})\), draw \(R_0\) with success probability \(\pi_1(L_0)\); set \(R_1=0\) whenever \(R_0=0\); and, conditional on \(R_0=1\), draw \(R_1\) with success probability \(\pi_2(L_1)\). Thus \(R_1\le R_0\) almost surely and the monotone-MAR identities are
\[
P(R_0=1\mid L_0,L_1,Y^{\rm MAR})=\pi_1(L_0)=P(R_0=1\mid L_0),
\]
\[
P(R_1=1\mid R_0=1,L_0,L_1,Y^{\rm MAR})=\pi_2(L_1)=P(R_1=1\mid R_0=1,L_0,L_1).
\]
Identify coordinates with the longitudinal observed law only on their observed events: \(L_0=H_1\), \(R_0=A_1\), \(L_1=H_2\) and \(R_1=A_2\) on \(A_1=1\), and the conditional law of \(Y^{\rm MAR}\) agrees with that of the longitudinal \(Y\) on \(A_1=A_2=1\). Equivalently, the auxiliary observed-data law of \( (R_0,L_0,R_0L_1,R_1,R_0R_1Y^{\rm MAR})\) agrees with the corresponding treated-regime coarsening of the longitudinal observed law. This auxiliary construction does not identify \(L_1\) with the causal potential state \(H_2(1)\) and imposes no additional state-consistency or other condition on the broad causal model.","inputs":["\(d\)","\(\alpha_1\)","\(t\)","\(M\)","\(c\)"]}

q(h_2)=\mathbf1\{h_{2,1}\le1/2\}-\mathbf1\{h_{2,1}>1/2\},\quad
a(h_1)=\cos(2\pi h_{1,1}),\quad
v(h_2)=\cos(4\pi h_{2,1}),
\]
extended constantly in the remaining coordinates, and fix small \(\kappa,\delta>0\). Define
\[
g_0(h_2\mid h_1)=1+\kappa a(h_1)q(h_2).
\]
With the same fixed off-regime and independent-uniform full-data laws, define the \(n\)-independent subclass
\[
\mathcal M_{\rm rough}
=\left\{P\in\mathcal M(\alpha_1,\alpha_2,\beta,t;M,c):
\begin{array}{l}
H_1\sim{\rm Unif}([0,1]^d),\ \pi_2=1/2,\ m_2=\delta v,\\

g^{\rm rough}_{z,\lambda}(h_2\mid h_1)
=g_0(h_2\mid h_1)+
\epsilon\sum_j\frac{\lambda_ju_{j,h}(h_1)v(h_2)}
 {1+2\eta z_ju_{j,h}(h_1)}.
\]

\[
F_j=2\eta(2A_1-1)u_{j,h}(H_1),\qquad
E_j=\epsilon\mathbf1\{A_1=1\}
\frac{u_{j,h}(H_1)v(H_2)}{g_0(H_2\mid H_1)}.
\]

\[
K_P(dy\mid l_0,l_1)
=P(Y\in dy\mid H_1=l_0,H_2=l_1,A_1=A_2=1)
\]
be a regular conditional probability kernel. Such a kernel exists because the state and outcome spaces are standard Borel; strict overlap makes the treated-regime conditioning well-defined on the \(p_1(l_0)g(l_1\mid l_0)\)-support, and an arbitrary measurable version may be fixed off that support. Define an auxiliary two-occasion full-data law by first drawing \((L_0,L_1)\) with density \(p_1(l_0)g(l_1\mid l_0)\), then drawing \(Y^{\rm MAR}\) from \(K_P(\cdot\mid L_0,L_1)\). P(R_0=1\mid L_0,L_1,Y^{\rm MAR})=\pi_1(L_0),

(R_0,L_0,R_0L_1,R_1,R_0R_1Y^{\rm MAR})
\overset{d}{=}
